narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, predicting their chances of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 school decile of 4, Year 3 decile of 8, and plans to attend full-time—and calculated a 48.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. Because this risk threshold was too high, your application was rejected to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 grades ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 grades ranking, in that order of impact. Your strong Year 3 decile of 8—well above the typical student's 5.53—stood out as the biggest positive force, pushing the prediction meaningfully toward expecting you to pass the bar. However, your LSAT score of 32, notably below the average of 36.7, had the strongest pull in the opposite direction, significantly raising the model's concern about bar failure. Your Year 1 decile of 4, lower than the average of 5.71, added a smaller but still concerning nudge toward the failure prediction. Compared to typical students, your later academic improvement is a bright spot that the model noticed and rewarded, but it wasn't quite enough to offset the earlier weaker performance and LSAT. Your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of applicants, so it didn't sway things much either way. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given your upward trajectory in grades, it highlights where targeted improvements could make a real difference. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as that was the dominant hurdle here. Strengthening your early academic record through additional coursework or explanations in a future application could also help. Many students in similar positions reapply successfully after addressing these key areas—your Year 3 strength shows you have the potential to turn this around.
